coredata.dc:title: Exploring pentagon-heptagon pair defects in the triangular graphene quantum dots: A computational study
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ghafouri, Reza
coredata.dc:description: We have applied density functional calculations to investigate Stone Wales (SW) and carbon ad-dimer (CD) defect formation in triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs). According to our results, defect formation energies depend on the positions of SW defects, such that the rotation of the CC bond located near the vertex of triangular GQD is easier than the rotation of other CC bonds. Therefore, the multiply defective GQDs with isolated SW defect sites are the most favorable while the formation of pentalene like structures in the connected SW defect sites costs larger formation energies. Introducing of carbon dimer defects on a triangular GQD induces a curvature at the defective sites, which leads to a more complex defect configuration with cone-like structure in the CD defective GQD with three defective sites. Then, formation energies for CD defective GQDs are higher than those for SW defective ones. The electrophilicity values calculated for SW and CD defective GQDs are greater than those for pristine GQDs. Moreover, perturbation of strong sp2 bonding network of graphitic carbons on the GQD, leading to the formation of more localized CC bonds, results in further electron deficiency of multiply SW and CD defective GQDs with increasing of electrophilicity values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructures
ab initio calculations
Defects
Electronic structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic structures of a) Stone-Wales (SW) and b) carbon ad-dimer (CD) defects, leading to the formation of a 5-7-7-5 and 7-5-5-7 ring patterns, respectively.
The representative patch LMN and geometry of the triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs).
Optimized structures of the SW defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs).
Optimized structures of the defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with two SW defects, SW(i, j). (i, j) indicates two rotated CC bonds with the coding numbers of bi and bj in Fig. 2.

Optimized structures of the defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with three SW defects, SW(i, j, k). (i, j, k) indicates three rotated CC bonds with the coding numbers of bi, bj and bk in Fig. 2.
Defect formation energies (ED in Kcal/mol) for the defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with three SW defects, SW(i, j, k).
The optimized structures of the CD defective GQDs with one, two and three defects.

Calculated density of spin-polarized states for perfect and the most energetically favorable configurations of SW defective GQDs with one, two and three defect sites, SW1, SW(1,1) and SW(1,1,1) together with their spatial distribution: red and green circles correspond to up (α) and down (β) spin.

Total energies (ET in a.u.), relative energies (Er in Kcal/mol), defect formation energies (ED in Kcal/mol), CC bond lengths of heptagon-heptagon junctions (h-h in Å), and electrophilicity index (ω) for SW defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

Total energies (ET in a.u.), relative energies (Er in Kcal/mol), defect formation energies per defect site (ED in Kcal/mol), and CC bond lengths of heptagon-heptagon junctions (h-h(i) and h-h(j) in Å) for defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with two SW defects, SW(i, j). (i, j) indicates two rotated CC bonds with the coding numbers of bi and bj in Fig. 2.

Total energies (ET in a.u.), defect formation energies per defect site (ED in Kcal/mol), CC bond lengths of heptagon-heptagon junctions (h-h(i) h-h(j) and h-h(k) in Å), and electrophilicity index (ω) for defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with three SW defects, SW(i, j, k). (i, j, k) indicates two rotated CC bonds with the coding numbers of bi, bj and bk in Fig. 2.

Total energies (ET in a.u.), defect formation energies per defect site (ED in Kcal/mol), CC bond lengths of pentagon-pentagon junctions (p-p in Å), and electrophilicity index (ω) for carbon ad-dimer defective triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

head.ce:title: Exploring pentagon-heptagon pair defects in the triangular graphene quantum dots: A computational study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar -e- Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre-Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have applied density functional calculations to investigate Stone Wales (SW) and carbon ad-dimer (CD) defect formation in triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs). According to our results, defect formation energies depend on the positions of SW defects, such that the rotation of the CC bond located near the vertex of triangular GQD is easier than the rotation of other CC bonds. Therefore, the multiply defective GQDs with isolated SW defect sites are the most favorable while the formation of pentalene like structures in the connected SW defect sites costs larger formation energies. Introducing of carbon dimer defects on a triangular GQD induces a curvature at the defective sites, which leads to a more complex defect configuration with cone-like structure in the CD defective GQD with three defective sites. Then, formation energies for CD defective GQDs are higher than those for SW defective ones. The electrophilicity values calculated for SW and CD defective GQDs are greater than those for pristine GQDs. Moreover, perturbation of strong sp2 bonding network of graphitic carbons on the GQD, leading to the formation of more localized CC bonds, results in further electron deficiency of multiply SW and CD defective GQDs with increasing of electrophilicity values.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanostructures have attracted lots of interest in condensed matter physics and material science due to their unique mechanical, optical, and electronic properties [1–3]. Among these carbon-based nanostructures, the low-dimensional monolayer sheets such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [4,5], graphene nanoflakes (GNFs) [6], and one-dimensional graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) [7–9] become one of the most exciting topics in recent research. Many unconventional electronic properties of these graphene nanostructures, such as metallic ferromagnetism and magnetic moments, make those a promising material for future nanoelectronics and nanodevices such as spin memory, transistors, solid-state qubits, and perhaps optoelectronics applications [10–14]. Wang et al. [6] characterized arbitrarily shaped hydrogen-terminated graphene nanoflakes with large spin using benzenoid graph theory and first-principles calculations. They demonstrate that the spin of a GNF depends on its shape due to topological frustration of the π-bonds. First principle calculations have also been performed to investigate the electronic properties of rectangular shaped graphenes, finite length GNRs [15,16], and the calculated results reveal that, in addition to edge quantum entrapment from ribbon width, finite size effects along the ribbon length affect the electronic states. Several theoretical calculations have been carried out to study the electronic properties and magnetism for different shaped zero-dimensional GQDs [17]. For example, Yu et al. [18] investigated the electronic properties of N/B doped triangular GQDs with hydrogen passivated zigzag edges in order to explore how N/B doping influences the electronic properties of GQDs. Then, Anafcheh et al. [19] investigated the magnetic properties of N/B doped triangular GQDs by computing the grid distribution of nucleus independent chemical shift (NICS) in order to monitoring shielding effects around molecule. Triangular GQDs with zigzag edges have degenerate zero-energy states and show metallic ferromagnetism, in which the net spin increases linearly with its size, indicating that the net magnetic moments in triangular GQDs are usually satisfied with Lieb's theorem [20].

Defects have been proposed to play a major role in the growth and subsequent annealing down to the structurally ordered ground state of carbon nanostructures. In fact, it is the second law of thermodynamics that dictates the presence of a certain amount of disorder in crystalline materials [21,22]. On the other word, it is thermodynamically impossible for defect densities to go to zero, even in highly pure crystalline systems. One might imagine that the strong sp2 bonding network of graphitic carbons would energetically preclude defect formation. But various studies have shown that atomic-scale intrinsic defects in carbon-based nanostructures can strongly influence the physical and chemical properties of such systems and can lead to interesting new applications [23–26]. The line and screw defects, interstitials, and bond rotation defects are all observed in carbon-based nanostructures in considerable numbers. Extensive literature has been developed around defects in carbon-based nanostructures, much of which is applicable to graphene-like structures [21,27,28]. It should be noted that in spite of the relevance theoretical works on GQDs so far, there has been no theoretical discussion on the defective GQDs in the literature. So, in the present work, we extend the study of the defects to triangular GQDs. A very important topological defect in carbon-based nanostructures is pentagon-heptagon pair, Stone-Wales (SW) [29] and carbon ad-dimer (CD) defect [30], leading to the formation of a 5-7-7-5 and 7-5-5-7 ring pattern, respectively, see Fig. 1 . The formation of these pentagon-heptagon pairs with 5-7-7-5 arrangement, Stone-Wales defect, can be considered to emerge from the regular hexagonal lattice by a 90° rotation of a CC bond which enables a pair of hexagons and pair of pentagons to switch places. In fact, it dates back to 1986, when Stone and Wales pointed out that there are many stable C60 isomers, which do not follow the well-known isolated pentagon rule, and which are related to the I
h form by transformations involving the rotation of two carbon atoms around the center of the bond connecting them [29]. In the 1999, Orlikowski et al. [30] introduced another pentagon-heptagon pair defect with the 7-5-5-7 arrangement, the carbon ad-dimer (CD) defect, which is obtained by the adsorption of a C2 dimer to a strained carbon nanotube (CNT). They proposed a mechanism based on it for forming tube-tube junctions. Sternberg et al. [31] reveals that for an unstrained tube the barrier for formation of the 7-5-5-7 (CD) defect is much lower than that for the 5-7-7-5 (SW) defect, and the former is also thermodynamically more favorable than the latter for most CNT sizes.

All density functional theory (DFT) calculations are performed using GAMESS suite of programs [32]. First, a selected triangular GQD with hydrogen passivated zigzag edges with size n = 6 is considered as a starting point for the design of defective GQDs. A triangle representative patch (LMN) is considered on the surface of the triangular GQD as being the smallest structure unit which is equal to 1/6 of the total surface, see Fig. 2 . In this representative model: C1 to C6 represent six different kinds of carbon atoms; b1 to b6 represent six distinct CC bonds. Geometries of the perfect, SW and CD defective GQDs are optimized at the unrestricted Becke three-parameter LeeYang-Parr (UB3LYP) methods with the 6-31G* basis set [33–35]. The standard 6-31G* basis set is employed for being affordable and accurate enough for geometry optimization of even large molecules [36,37]. The optimized structures of SW and CD defective GQDs and their parent are subjected to the calculation of the related properties. The optimized structures of defective GQDs are shown in Figs. 3–5 and 7
.

Triangular GQD with zigzag edge size n = 6, which is consisted of 61 carbon atoms and 21 hydrogen atoms, is fully optimized at the UB3LYP/6-31G* level, as the parent molecule for defective GQDs. The CC bond lengths of prefect triangular GQDs are predicted to be 1.420–1.437 Å, in agreement with those reported for monolayer sheets of carbon (1.42 Å) [9]. We select six different CC bonds at the GQD according to the symmetry of the triangular structure, b1-b6, see Fig. 2. Therefore, in our work, the considered SW defective GQDs are denoted by SW1- SW6. Fig. 3 shows the optimized structures of the GQDs with one SW defective site while the total energies (ET), relative energies (Erel) and defect formation energies (ED) of these models are given in Table 1 along with the values for their parent. The rotated CC bond lengths of heptagon-heptagon junctions are obtained to be 1.351–1.385 Å in the SW defective GQDs, which are larger than those of reported by Lu et al. [38] for SW defects in the nanoribons.

The results containing in Table 1 present two sets of the relative energies for SW defective GQDs. In the first set, SW1 is the most stable configuration followed by SW2, SW6, and SW3 while for the two other SW defective GQDs, SW4 and SW5 models, in which defects are produced by the rotation of CC bonds in the inner area of GQD, relative energies have larger values. For further investigation, defect formation energies (the energies required to form SW defects) are calculated using the following equation:
(1) ED = Edefective – Eperfect
where Edefective and Eperfect are the total energies of the SW defective GQDs and perfect GQD, respectively. It can be seen that defect formation energies for the SW defective GQDs range between 64.81 and 83.95 kcal/mol (endothermic character), which are smaller than those reported for SW defect formation in the graphene [38]. Among all the SW defective GQDs, defect formation energy for the most stable isomer, SW1, has the smallest value, meaning that the rotation of CC the bond that is located along the height and near the vertex of triangle is easier than the rotation of the other CC bonds. On the other hand, the largest value of defect formation energy is obtained for the SW5 in which SW defect is produced by the rotation of the CC bond along the height in the center of GQD. Therefore, it seems that the formation of SW defect is sensitive to the position of rotating CC bond on the GQD surface.

To verify the effect of size on the defect formation energy, we further considered a triangular GQD with zigzag edge size n = 8, which is consisted of 97 carbon atoms and 27 hydrogen atoms, is fully optimized at the same level of theory; the obtained results are illustrated in the supplementary material (Table S1 and Fig. S1). It is worth noting that defect formation energies for the SW defective GQDs with zigzag edge size n = 8 range between 62.21 and 82.44 kcal/mol, which are similar to those values obtained for the SW defective GQDs with zigzag edge size n = 6 (61 carbon atoms and 21 hydrogen atoms) [38]. Moreover, it is seen that the rotation of CC bond that is located along the height and near the vertex of triangle is easier than the rotation of the other CC bonds. On the other word, the largest value of defect formation energy is obtained when SW defect is produced by the rotation of the CC bond along the height in the center of GQD.

Fig. 4 shows the optimized structures of the defective GQDs with two SW defects. For the sake of convenience, each isomer of defective GQD with two SW defects is designated as SW(i, j), indicating that two CC bonds with the coding numbers of bi and bj are rotated, see Fig. 2. For example, SW(1, 1) represents an isomer of SW defective GQD in which two CC bonds with the coding numbers of b1 are rotated by a 90° for the formation of two 7-5-5-7 defects. Defect formation energies (ED) and relative energies (Erel) of these isomers are given in Table 2 . Our DFT results indicate that the atomic arrangement of the isomer where two SW defects are located near the vertexes of triangle, SW(1, 1), makes the most energetically favorable configuration, with the defect formation energy of 74.52 kcal/mol per each defect site, followed by SW(1, 2) and SW(2, 2). On the other hand, the rotation of two CC bonds both with the coding numbers of b6, SW(6, 6), CC bonds with the coding numbers of b4 and b6, SW(4, 6), and b2 and b5, SW(2, 5), which lead to the formation of two fused pentagons (pentalene like structure) in the SW defective GQDs, make the largest defect formation energies by about 107.82, 103.63 and 100.81 kcal/mol per each defect site, respectively, see Table 2. The geometrical parameters of newly formed defects are close to those obtained for the defective GQDs with one SW defect discussed above. For example, the prediction of bond lengths of heptagon-heptagon and heptagon -pentagon junctions in the defective GQDs with two SW defective sites are obtained to be 1.344–1.383 and 1.410–1.498 Å, respectively, consistent with the values of 1.35 and 1.46 Å reported by Lu et al. [38] for SW defects in the graphene.

One of the unique properties of the graphitic network with sp2-hybridized carbon atoms is its ability to reconstruct by forming nonhexagonal rings. In this study, we consider 19 configurations for GQDs with three SW defects, SW(i, j, k). Fig. 5 shows the optimized structures of the SW defective GQDs with three SW defects. The defect formation energies of the considered SW defective GQDs strongly depends on the position of SW defects. Fig. 6 depicts the trend of the formation energies per defect for the SW defective GQDs with three SW defects. Taking a quick glance at the results, it can be seen that the defect formation energies of the entire series may be divided into three zones: SW defective GQDs with isolated SW defect sites, 82.33–90.40 kcal/mol, SW defective GQDs with joined SW defect sites, 100.08–111.61 kcal/mol, and SW defective GQDs with connected pentagonal rings (pentalene like structures), 119.45–129.37 kcal/mol. As seen from Table 3 , the rotation of three CC bonds with the coding numbers of 1 in the SW(1, 1, 1) leads to the formation of three isolated SW defect sites in each vertex of GQD with defect formation energy of about 82.33 kcal/mol per defect. The formation energy of this SW defective GQD is about 5.30 and 8.11 kcal/mol lower than that of SW(2, 2, 1) and SW(1, 1, 2), respectively. The largest value of defect formation energy is related to the SW(2, 2, 5) which have two pentalene like structures. It can be due to the fact that the contraction of pentagon-pentagon bond lengths in the pentalene like structures, ranging from 1.378 to 1.395 Å, induces a structural bump which results in the instability in the considered SW defective GQDs.

In order to further investigation, it would be interesting to compare the defect formation energies of these SW defective GQDs with similar CD defective ones. Fig. 7 shows the optimized structures of the CD defective GQDs with one, two and three defect sites. The 7-5-5-7 arrangement of heptagon and pentagon rings in the CD defective GQDs induces a local curvature at the defective sites. As mentioned above, the CD defect is formed on a perfect hexagonal network by adsorption of a carbon dimer which introduces two adjacent pentagons between two heptagons, see Fig. 1. Then, it is assumed that the reaction presented in Eq. (2) occurs:
(2) GQD + CC → CD – GQD
where GQD and CD-GQD represent the perfect and CD defective GQDs, respectively. Defect formation energies (the energies required to form ad-dimer defects) are defined as:
(3)
E D = E CD-GQD – E GQD – 2E C
where E CD-GQD and E GQD are the total energy of a defective GQD and that of perfect GQD, respectively. As seen in Table 4 , the formation energy of one CD defect on the triangular GQD is obtained to be 98.47 kcal/mol which is smaller than the formation energies reported for nanotubes [31]. Formation energy for defective triangular GQD with three CD defects, CD(1, 1, 1), is obtained to be 112.31 kcal/mol per CD defect, higher than those for defective GQDs with three isolated SW defects. In fact, introducing of three carbon dimers induces a local curvature at the defective sites on the triangular GQD which lead to more complex defect configuration with cone-like structure.

The efficiency of a molecule as an electron acceptor depends not only on its electron affinity in order to effectively absorb electron from adjacent donor molecules, but also on its resistance against electron back transfer to donor molecule. To this aim a global index has been defined by Parr et al. [39] in terms of quantitative chemical concepts in DFT which simultaneously considers electron affinity of a molecule and its resistance to exchange electron [40]. Since the most of the reactions can be analyzed through the electrophilicity of various species involved, a proper understanding of these properties becomes essential. Anafcheh et al. [41] used the electrophilicity index to investigate the propensity of the C60 and C70 fullerene derivatives to acquire an electron, as acceptor in bulk heterojunction solar cells and (SiH)48X12 heterofullerenes with the group III and V dopants [42]. Such quantity can be assigned as a basis for evaluating the electrophilic competence of a system by applying μ as a reliable factor for measuring propensity of the system to acquire an additional electron from adjacent electron-rich species, and simultaneously using η for describing the resistance of the system to exchange electron with the environment. Therefore, the electrophilicity index is given by Ref. [40].
(4) ω = μ2/2η
in which electronic chemical potential (μ) and hardness (η) could be given by
(5) μ = −(IP + EA)/2
(6) η = IP – EA

The IP = E(M+) – E(M) and the EA = E(M) – E(M–) values are the computed vertical ionization potentials and electron affinities. It is worth noting that these relations are just approximations for μ and η. Therefore, ω values are calculated for the SW and CD defective GQDs from the above mentioned formula. It would be interesting to compare the calculated electrophilicity of SW defective GQDs with that of perfect GQD. The results (Table 1) indicate that the defects lead to an increase in the electrophilicity values; the electrophilicity values of the SW defective GQDs are greater than that of perfect GQD, 2.24 eV. Further, the electrophilicity values of the multiply SW defective GQDs (with three defects, Table 3) are always greater than those of SW defective GQDs with one SW defect. This may relates to the formation of more localized CC bonds and perturbation of strong sp2 bonding network of graphitic carbons on the GQD surface, which leads to the enhancement of electron deficiency of SW defective GQDs. Therefore, as expected CD defective GQDs with cone-like structures (Table 4) have larger electrophilicity in comparison to the SW defective ones.

Graphene is a semi-metal at the micron scale or larger, but when it is trimmed down to less than 100 nm, electron confinement opens its band-gap, resulting in an effect that can be used to tune the crystal's electronics for different purposes. Among these low-dimensional carbon-based nanostructures, triangular GQDs have attracted enormous attention due to their magnetic and electronic properties that are expected to be important for future application in nanoelectronics [43]. It is well known that the mechanical, magnetic and electronic properties of carbon-based nanostructures can be controlled through the incorporation of impurities or the presence of defects [21]. In addition, due to their flat structure, GQDs are much easier to manipulate than other carbon-based nanostructures such as nanotubes.

The honeycomb lattice of graphene is formed by two triangular interpenetrating sublattices, A and B, see Fig. 1. The edge imbalance in triangular GQDs results in a global imbalance, so that the total number of atoms in the sublattice A and B (NA and NB) is not the same. Previous theoretical studies have revealed that the electronic and magnetic properties of GQDs are related to this sublattice imbalance in agreement with Lieb's theorem [20]. In fact, triangular GQDs have spin-polarized ground states. The total spin of the ground state in a bipartite lattice is 2S = N
A
-N
B and that is mainly localized on the edges. This could have been anticipated from Hund's rule and the appearance, in the noninteracting model, of NA – NB degenerate states with strictly zero energy. In other words, spin polarization of GQDs results from the strict zero-energy states and the absence of kinetic energy penalty in sublattice unbalanced graphene structures.

Due to the existence of the magnetic moment at the edges of SW defective GQDs, the spin-polarized DFT is employed, whose electronic structures are plotted in Fig. 8 . This figure illustrates a comparison between densities of spin-polarized states (spin-up and spin-down) of: (a) perfect GQD, (b) SW1, (c) SW(1, 1), and (d) SW(1, 1, 1), as the most energetically favorable configurations along with the corresponding spatial distribution of the spin density. The densities of states (DOS) of perfect GQD coincide with those reported for zigzag-edged triangular graphene nanoflakes by Wang et al. [6] Energy gaps between the singly occupied molecular orbitals (SOMOs) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs), Eg, are calculated from the density of state results. The energy gaps of the perfect GQD are obtained to be 1.24 and 1.22 eV for the α-spin and β-spin branch, respectively, consistent with previous theoretical results for zigzag-edged triangular GNFs [6,11]. In the most stable configurations, SW1, SW(1, 1), and SW(1, 1, 1) models, the α-spin branches have energy gaps of 1.11, 1.05, and 0.90 eV, while there are small energy gaps of 0.77, 0.77 and 0.80 eV in the β-spin branches, respectively. As seen, SW defective GQDs have an intriguing energy spectrum close to the Fermi level. Two striking peaks near the Fermi level in the two spin states (α and β spin) can be understood as those of free massless electrons confined in a triangular cavity. Our DFT calculations indicate that the ground states of SW defective GQDs exhibit magnetic properties similar to those of GQDs. The splitting between spin-up and spin-down orbital states around the Fermi level demonstrates that GQDs are ferromagnetic, which is consistent with previous calculations for zigzag-edged graphene nanoribons. As seen from Fig. 8, in the considered GQDs electrons prefer to reside on defective sites. Therefore, the half-filling occupation criterion in Lieb's law is not satisfied in SW defective GQDs [20]. The failure of Lieb's law in triangular SW defective GQDs can be attributed to this fact that in SW defective GQDs, the rotation of CC bonds perturbs the honeycomb lattice of GQDs and orbitals of the SW defective carbons have different on-site energies in two sublattices. Similar changes are observed for the CD defective GQDs with one, two, and three defect sites, CD1, CD(1, 1), and CD(1, 1, 1). Generally, our DFT results imply that the energy gaps of GQDs may be modified at the presence of defects. These interesting electronic properties will make defective GQDs promising materials for many potential applications, particularly in nanoelectronics.

We have investigated Stone Wales (SW) and carbon ad-dimer (CD) defect formation in triangular graphene quantum dots at the UB3LYP/6-31G* level. Some interesting results are found as follows. (i) Our DFT results indicate that defect formation energies depend on the positions of SW defects, such that the rotation of CC bond that is located along the height and near the vertex of triangle is easier than the rotation of other CC bonds. (ii) The defective GQDs with isolated SW defect sites are the most favorable while the formation of pentalene like structures in the connected SW defect sites costs larger formation energies. (iii) The 7-5-5-7 arrangement of heptagon and pentagon rings in the CD defective GQDs induces a local curvature at the defective sites which leads to a more complex defective configuration with cone-like structure in the CD defective GQD with three defect sites. (iv) Formation energies for CD defective GQDs are always higher than SW defective ones. (v) Defects lead to an increase in the electrophilicity and electron deficiency of GQDs. (vi) Electrophilicity values of the multiply defective GQDs (with three defects) are always greater than those of defective GQDs with one defect. (vii) CD defective GQDs with cone-like structures have larger electrophilicity in comparison to the SW defective ones.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2016.03.025.
